one.en: PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where the sixth respondent, who is the adult child of the husband and wife, seeks an order removing his current litigation gu…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where the sixth respondent, who is the adult child of the husband and wife, seeks an order removing his current litigation guardian and appointing his sister as litigation guardian in her place, on the basis that the wife no longer has the funds to meet the fees of the current litigation guardian – Where the application is opposed by the husband – Where the husband contended that the sixth respondent no longer needs a litigation guardian – Where the medical evidence clearly establishes that the sixth respondent requires a litigation guardian – Where the husband’s recent opposition raises questions as to the bona fides of that opposition – Where the Court is satisfied that the sister of the sixth respondent is able to competently conduct the proceedings on his behalf – Orders made for the removal of the current litigation guardian and for the sister of the sixth respondent to be appointed. FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the sixth respondent seeks that the husband pay his costs on an indemnity basis, or in the alternative on a scale basis – Where the husband opposes any order for costs and contends that each party should bear their own costs – Where the circumstances are not exceptional such as to warrant an indemnity costs order – Costs ordered in a fixed sum.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

By an Application in a Proceeding filed on 20 February 2026, the sixth respondent in the substantive proceedings, by his litigation guardian Ms AS, sought orders that she be removed as litigation guardian and that Ms C Eccheli, the sister of the sixth respondent, be appointed in her place.
The application was opposed by the applicant in the substantive proceedings (“the husband”).
Following the hearing of the application on 18 March 2026, I made the following orders:
THE COURT ORDERS THAT:
Pursuant to Rule 3.15 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth):
(a) [Ms AS] is removed as the Litigation Guardian on behalf of [Mr P] and

(b) That in replacement, [Ms C Eccheli] is appointed the Litigation Guardian on behalf of [Mr P].

2. Leave is granted to the sixth respondent to make an oral application for costs.
The respondent husband pay the costs of the sixth respondent in bringing this application; such costs to be fixed in the sum of $5,500 and be paid within 42 days.
All extant applications and responses in relation to this issue relating to the appointment of a litigation guardian are otherwise dismissed.

AND THE COURT NOTES THAT otherwise the matter is before the court on 31 July 2026 for the purposes of a compliance check for the final hearing.

AND THE COURT DIRECTS THAT:
As a consequence of changes in the judicial calendar that date is vacated and the matter is listed for an in-person mention at 9.30 am on 10 August 2026 in lieu thereof.
These reasons explain the making of those orders.

BACKGROUND

The sixth respondent and others are engaged in highly conflictual litigation pursuant to Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The degree of conflict is reflected in the fact that some 12 judgements have been delivered to date, while the digital court file records over 300 documents filed and 48 sets of orders made. Several interlocutory applications remain extant and are listed before another judge for a two-day hearing, and the matter itself is listed for a 10-day final hearing in December 2026.
In 2025, proceedings were commenced in the Supreme Court of New South Wales by AK Pty Ltd against Mr P, the sixth respondent. The Statement of Claim contended that Mr P held property on trust for the plaintiff.
Mr P, by a Defence to Statement of Claim filed on his behalf by his then tutor in those Supreme Court proceedings, contended as follows:
2. In answer to paragraph 2 of the SOC, [Mr P] states that:
(a) He is 26 years old, born in [1999].
(b) He is the third child and the son of the parties to the marriage in these Federal Circuit & Family Court of Australia proceedings (FCFCOA Proceedings).

(c) He has at all material times had Autism Spectrum Disorder - Asperger's Syndrome, Major Depression and Generalised Anxiety Disorder as medical conditions that affect his mental capacity.

(d) He has at all material times been affected in his mental capacity as described in the Report of [Dr U] in his report dated [...] 2025 as annexed to her affidavit dated [...] 2025, filed in the NSWSC Proceedings in [2025].

(e) In the circumstances of his medical condition in (c) and (d) above, he has required [Ms AV] to act as his Tutor in the NSWSC Proceedings and as Litigation Guardian in these FCFCOA Proceedings to conduct his defence of the allegations in the SOC.

(f) By reason of the facts above, [Mr P] was, at all material times, in a position of special vulnerability, vis a vis, [AK Pty Ltd] and his father, [Mr Eccheli].

(g) His father, [Mr Eccheli], the Applicant in these FCFCOA Proceedings has at all material times been aware of the facts in paragraph 1 and 2 above.

(h) Was on 20 October 2025, joined as the sixth respondent to the FCFCOA Proceedings.

As paragraph (d) above records, the application for the appointment of a tutor was supported by a medical report of Dr U dated 19 July 2025.
On 20 October 2025, Mr P, by his litigation guardian, was joined to these Pt VIII proceedings with the consent of all parties, including the husband. At that stage, the husband did not then contend (as he now does) that the sixth respondent did not require a litigation guardian.
On 24 November 2025, orders were made that the then litigation guardian for the sixth respondent be removed and replaced by Ms AS as litigation guardian. Consistent with the position taken by the husband on 20 October 2025, he did not contend that the sixth respondent did not require a litigation guardian.
In her affidavit filed in support of the present application, the litigation guardian states that she accepted her appointment on the basis that her legal fees would be paid. She says that on 16 January 2026 she was informed by the wife that she did not have the funds to continue to pay her fees and accordingly seeks to be removed.
In her place, the sixth respondent’s sister, Ms C Eccheli, seeks to be appointed litigation guardian.
In her affidavit in support of that application, she states that she is the daughter of the husband and wife and sister of the sixth respondent. She also says that she was previously a party to the proceedings but is no longer.
Her affidavit goes on to say:
[Mr P] lives with my mother, and I have spoken to both [Mr P] and my mother about [Mr P’s] litigation guardian. I understand from conversations with them that [Mr P] cannot pay his own legal fees or the costs. of the litigation guardian and my mother has told me words to the effect of "I cannot meet [Mr P’s] legal fees right now or pay his litigation guardian".
I have told [Mr P] and his solicitor that I am prepared to act as his litigation guardian.
19. I do not require payment to act in this role.
I consent to my appointment to act as [Mr P’s] Litigation Guardian in these proceedings.
I do not have an interest in these proceedings adverse to the interests of [Mr P].

...
I understand that should Orders be made appointing me as [Mr P’s] Litigation Guardian, that I must do anything required to be done by [Mr P]. pursuant to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021.
I understand that the duty of disclosure applies to me as [Mr P’s] Litigation Guardian, on behalf of [Mr P].

(Affidavit of [Ms C Eccheli]filed 20 February 2026)
The husband in his affidavit, Outline of Case Document and through the submissions of his counsel, contends that the sixth respondent does not require a litigation guardian; that the medical report relied upon in the Supreme Court proceedings does not establish the need for a litigation guardian; that the proposed appointment of Ms C Eccheli as litigation guardian would be adverse to his interests; that Ms C Eccheli may be required to give evidence in these proceedings; that she has acted in a manner that has reduced the pool of assets; and that, despite her assertions to the contrary, the wife has the capacity to meet the legal fees of the existing litigation guardian.
He further submits that, if the Court determines that a litigation guardian is necessary, two individuals who are neighbours of the wife should instead be appointed as litigation guardian.

APPLICABLE LAW

Part 3.5 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) sets out the criteria and method for the appointment of a litigation guardian. Rules 3.14 and 3.15 provides as follows:
3.14 Who may be a litigation guardian

A person may be a litigation guardian in a proceeding if the person:
(a) in the case of an individual—is an adult; and

(b) in any case:
(i) has no interest in the proceeding adverse to the interest of the person needing the litigation guardian; and

(ii) can fairly and competently conduct the proceeding for the person needing the litigation guardian.

3.15 Appointment of litigation guardian
(1) A person may apply for the appointment, replacement or removal of a person as the litigation guardian of a party.

(2) The court may, at the request of a party or on its own initiative, appoint or remove a litigation guardian, or substitute another person as litigation guardian, in a proceeding in the interests of a person who needs a litigation guardian.

(3) A person becomes a litigation guardian if the person consents to the appointment by filing an affidavit of consent in the proceeding.

(4) The court may, on the filing of an affidavit, remove a litigation guardian at the written request of the litigation guardian.

In Logan & Logan [2016] FamCA 649 (“Logan & Logan”), Berman J observed, in the context of the appointment of a case guardian (being the terminology used under an earlier iteration of the Rules), as follows:
The position of a case guardian does not need to be neutral or impartial. He can be highly protective of the protected person and even strongly opposed to the opposing party. Providing he acts honestly and is seen to do so thereby protecting the interests of the protected person, there can be no challenge to his retention.
It is not necessarily that a case guardian will have his position terminated and the appointment discharged simply because he may stand to gain by the outcome of the litigation. As was considered by the Full Court in Kannis & Kannis [2002] FamCA 1150; (2003) FLC 93-135:
The fact that the next friend for the husband stood to gain if his father obtained a more favourable result [than] that sought by the wife could not be seen as a reason to remove him. Indeed to the contrary. There was some communality of interest between the next friend and his father. The fact that the next friend proved to be an unreliable witness would not be enough to make it appropriate to remove him.

DISCUSSION

I am satisfied that there is no merit to the husband’s opposition to the application for the following reasons:
(a) The husband’s contention that the medical report of Dr U does not establish that the sixth respondent requires a litigation guardian is arrant nonsense. The medical report dated 19 July 2025 (annexed to the husband’s affidavit) records the following:
[Mr P] has attended this clinic regularly for the past four years. I have been his regular GP throughout the period and am therefore well acquainted with his medical history, current condition, and ongoing treatment.

...

2. Capacity to give instructions & engage in proceedings

[Mr P] can grasp in broad outline the nature, purpose and general possible outcomes of the litigation. However, he has marked limitations in retaining detail, sequencing events accurately, and integrating information across issues. Each concept must be broken down into single, concrete steps and often reframed as binary ("yes/no") choices.

He requires:

(1) repetition of questions and explanations

(2) visual scaffolds (simple diagrams / flow charts)

(3) substantially extended processing time before he can indicate a preference. Even with these supports his ability to synthesise cumulative advice (e.g. combining risk, cost, evidentiary and strategic considerations) is poor. He cannot reliably formulate or revise an overall risk-benefit appraisal.

When presented with detailed advice he becomes anxious, his processing speed slows, and he tends to defer or shut down. He struggles to appreciate how a decision on one issue affects downstream options on others without re-explanation each time. Free-form or open-ended questioning yields minimal usable instruction; structured yes/no prompts are usually required for any response.

In functional terms, he is unable to autonomously manage or sustain the iterative , integrative decision-making demanded by Supreme Court processes and would require continuous, structured facilitation far beyond ordinary solicitor-client interactions.

...

Overall While [Mr P] displays simplified conceptual understanding when heavily scaffolded, he lacks the functional capacity to manage the ongoing, integrative instruction process necessary for court proceedings at this level.

...

Overall opinion

Although [Mr P] demonstrates formal capacity in controlled, short interactions, the functional impact of his anxiety and limited attention span means he is effectively incompetent to give evidence under standard court conditions. Continuous support from familiar persons would not overcome the duration and intensity demands of Supreme Court processes.

(Annexure Z of the Affidavit of [Mr Eccheli] filed 17 March 2026)
(b) The medical evidence clearly establishes that the sixth respondent requires a litigation guardian.

(c) At no time prior to filing his Response to an Application in a Proceeding in March this year did the husband raise any issue about the sixth respondent’s need for a litigation guardian. His recent opposition raises questions about the bona fides of his objection.

(d) Where the existing litigation guardian seeks to be removed, any suggestion that the wife has the capacity to meet the legal fees of the litigation guardian is otiose. The evidence is that the litigation guardian seeks to be removed and there is no evidence that she wishes to remain in that role even if her fees are paid. In those circumstances, the husband’s focus in his affidavit and during submissions on the wife’s alleged financial capacity reinforces the view that his opposition lacks bona fides, particularly when contrasted with his own assertions of impecuniosity, which appear inconsistent with what is asserted in his costs notice.

(e) The husband’s assertions that Ms C Eccheli’s interests are adverse to his, or that she maybe a witness in the proceedings, are irrelevant considerations (see Logan & Logan).

(f) The husband’s proposed alterative litigation guardians have not provided the requisite consent to act.

I am satisfied that the medical evidence establishes the need for the appointment of a litigation guardian. I am further satisfied that the requirements of r 3.14 of the Rules are met. Ms C Eccheli is an adult, has no interests in the proceedings adverse to those of the sixth respondent and is able to fairly and competently conduct the proceedings on his behalf.

COSTS

The sixth respondent sought an order for his costs in the sum of $10,000 plus GST, contending that this amount represented his costs on an indemnity basis. On a scale basis, costs were sought in the sum of $7,000 plus GST.
The husband opposed an order for costs, contending that each party should pay their own costs.
An application for costs is governed by the provisions of s 114UB of the Act which provides a general rule that each party to the proceedings should bear their own costs. Pursuant to s 114UB(2) of the Act, the Court may, in its discretion, make such order as to costs as the circumstances justify having regard to the matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act.
No one factor in the list of factors set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act is determinative and the Court may give such weight as it considers relevant to any factor (Fitzgerald v Fish [2005] FamCA 158; (2005) 33 Fam LR 123 at [130]).
Addressing the matters under s 114UB(3) of the Act, insofar as they are relevant, I am satisfied on the material before me that the sixth respondent has limited financial means. The reasons for judgment anonymised as Eccheli & Eccheli (No 7) [2025] FedCFamC1F 333 and Eccheli & Eccheli (No 11) [2025] FedCFamC1F 671 lend weight to the submission that the husband’s financial circumstances are less than opaque. I am satisfied the husband has the capacity to meet an order for costs. In that regard, I note the cost notice filed by the husband on 17 March 2026 provides as follows:
YOUR FUTURE ESTIMATED COSTS	

Hearing opposing [Ms C Eccheli] as litigation guardian 18 March 2026	

Solicitor preparation and attendance	$2200
Counsel preparation and attendance at Hearing	E$3300
Total	E$5500
...
The source of the funds for the costs to be paid to Zali Burrows at Law and Counsel fees (SC and Junior Counsel)	

Husband seeks funding from other sources to pay legal fees for solicitor and Counsel, including an application for funds held in the controlled monies account be released to the Husband heard 23 and 24 March 2026. The course of funds also to come from consulting fees, loans from family and inheritance from mother’s estate once distributed.	E$900,000
The husband sets out that the projected future funding of $900,000 will be able to be met in part through consulting fees, loans from family members and an expected inheritance from his mother’s estate. I am satisfied that the husband has the capacity to meet the order sought. Additionally, the sixth respondent was put to unnecessary expense in having to bring this application, and the husband’s opposition lacked merit and bona fides, such that the sixth respondent should be compensated for the costs incurred.
For the above reasons I am satisfied that a costs order should be made. The authorities make it plain that an order for indemnity costs is not one that is made lightly and there should be circumstances of an exceptional kind that warrant costs on an indemnity basis (Kohan and Kohan [1992] FamCA 116; (1993) FLC 92-340). Despite my view as to a lack of bona fides in the husband’s opposition to the application, and that his opposition was without merit, I am not satisfied that the circumstances are exceptional.
It is clear that the Court has power to fix an amount for costs (Pascoe & Larsen (No 2) [2022] FedCFamC1A 126; Graham & Squibb [2019] FamCAFC 33; (2019) FLC 93-892). I propose to adopt the approach taken by the Full Court in Warbrick & Warbrick (No 2) (2021) FLC 94-030, where the Full Court observed at [32] that it did “not intend to permit the costs question to become, in effect, satellite litigation” and therefore would fix the costs amount.
I am satisfied that a proper amount for the sixth respondent’s costs is $5,500. In that respect, I note that is the same amount represented by the husband’s solicitor to be the husband’s own costs of the application. In those circumstances, he cannot be heard to complain that the amount is unreasonable or excessive.
